title: CPT: Colorful Prompt Tuning for Pre-trained Vision-Language Models

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## - CPT: COLORFUL PROMPT TUNING FOR PRE TRAINED VISION-LANGUAGE MODELS

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Pre-Trained Vision-Language Models (VL-PTMs) have shown promising capabilities in grounding natural language in image data, facilitating a broad variety of cross-modal tasks. However, we note that there exists a significant gap between the objective forms of model pre-training and fine-tuning, resulting in a need for large amounts of labeled data to stimulate the visual grounding capability of VLPTMs for downstream tasks. To address the challenge, we present Cross-modal Prompt Tuning (CPT, alternatively, Colorful Prompt Tuning), a novel paradigm for tuning VL-PTMs, which reformulates visual grounding into a fill-in-the-blank problem with color-based co-referential markers in image and text, maximally mitigating the gap. In this way, CPT enables strong few-shot and even zero-shot visual grounding capabilities of VL-PTMs. Comprehensive experimental results show that the prompt-tuned VL-PTMs outperform their fine-tuned counterparts by a large margin (e.g., 17 _._ 3% absolute accuracy improvement, and 73 _._ 8% relative standard deviation reduction on average with one shot in RefCOCO evaluation). All the data and codes will be available to facilitate future research.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Grounding natural language in fine-grained image regions is essential for a broad variety of visionlanguage tasks, such as robotic navigation (Tellex et al., 2011; Anderson et al., 2018b), visual question answering (Antol et al., 2015; Anderson et al., 2018a), visual dialogue (Das et al., 2017), and visual commonsense reasoning (Zellers et al., 2019). Recently Pre-Trained Vision-Language Models (VL-PTMs) have shown promising capabilities in visual grounding. Typically, generic cross-modal representations are first pre-trained on large-scale image-caption data in a self-supervised fashion, and then fine-tuned to adapt to downstream tasks (Lu et al., 2019; Su et al., 2019; Li et al., 2020; Radford et al., 2021). This _pre-training-then-fine-tuning_ paradigm of VL-PTMs has greatly pushed forward the state-of-the-art of many cross-modal tasks.

Despite the success, we note that there exists a significant gap between the objective forms of pretraining and fine-tuning of VL-PTMs. As illustrated in Figure 1, during pre-training, most VL-PTMs are optimized based on the masked language modeling objective, trying to recover the masked token from the cross-modal context. However, during fine-tuning, downstream tasks are usually conducted by classifying unmasked token representations into semantic labels, where task-specific parameters are typically introduced. The gap hinders the effective adaptation of VL-PTMs to downstream tasks. As a result, a large amount of labeled data is typically required to stimulate the visual grounding capabilities of VL-PTMs for downstream tasks.

In this work, inspired by recent progress in pre-trained language models in natural language processing (Brown et al., 2020; Schick & Sch¨utze, 2021a; Liu et al., 2021), we present Cross-modal Prompt Tuning (CPT, alternatively, Colorful Prompt Tuning), a novel paradigm for tuning VLPTMs. The key insight is that by adding color-based co-referential markers in both image and text, visual grounding can be reformulated into a fill-in-the-blank problem, maximally mitigating the gap between pre-training and fine-tuning. As shown in Figure 1, to ground natural language expressions in image data, CPT consists of two components: (1) a _visual sub-prompt_ that uniquely marks image regions with colored blocks or segmentation masks, and (2) a _textual sub-prompt_ that puts the query text into a color-based query template. Explicit grounding to the target image region can then be achieved by recovering the corresponding color text from the masked token in the query

Only square parts of relevant image regions are shown for illustration.
template. In addition, we present a principled method to search for high-quality cross-modal prompt configurations (i.e., visual appearances and texts of colors) for CPT.

By mitigating the gap from pre-training, CPT enables strong few-shot and even zero-shot visual grounding capabilities of VL-PTMs. Experimental results show that the prompt-tuned VL-PTMs outperform their fine-tuned counterparts by a large margin. For example, using colored blocks as visual sub-prompts, CPT achieves 17 _._ 3% absolute accuracy improvement, and 73 _._ 8% relative standard deviation reduction on average with one shot in RefCOCO evaluation. In the same setting, when equipped with colored segmentation masks as visual sub-prompts, CPT can further achieve 20 _._ 0% absolute accuracy improvement, and 76 _._ 2% relative standard deviation reduction than the vanilla fine-tuning approach.

Our contributions are summarized as threefold: (1) We present a novel cross-modal prompt tuning paradigm for VL-PTMs. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first attempt in both cross-modal prompt tuning for VL-PTMs, and zero- and few-shot visual grounding independent of object types. (2) We present a principled approach to search for high-quality cross-modal prompt configurations for CPT. (3) We conduct comprehensive experiments which demonstrate the effectiveness of CPT.

## 2 PRELIMINARY

In the literature, visual grounding is typically formulated as a referring expression comprehension (REC) problem (Plummer et al., 2015; Mao et al., 2016). Given an image _I_ and a query text of referring expression _q_ , REC aims to locate the target region in _I_ that corresponds to _q_ . In this section, we introduce the vanilla fine-tuning approach for VL-PTMs.

A common practice for REC is to first detect a set of region proposals _{v_ 1 _, v_ 2 _, . . . , vn}_ via object detectors, and then classify or rank the proposals to select the target region (Lu et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2020). Specifically, visual and textual inputs are first transformed into a sequence of input tokens _{_ [IMG] _, v_ 1 _, v_ 2 _, . . . , vn,_ [CLS] _, w_ 1 _, w_ 2 _, . . . , wm,_ [SEP] _}_ , where _{w_ 1 _, w_ 2 _, . . . , wm}_ are textual tokens of _q_ , and [IMG], [CLS] and [SEP] are special tokens. To obtain input representations, the feature of image regions is extracted by visual encoders, and the embeddings of textual and special tokens are obtained by a lookup table. Then input representations are fed into the pre-trained transformers to produce the hidden representations _{_ **h** [IMG] _,_ **h**[1] _v[,]_ **[ h]**[2] _v[, . . . ,]_ **[ h]** _[n] v[,]_ **[ h]**[[CLS]] _[,]_ **[ h]**[1] _w[,]_ **[ h]**[2] _w[, . . . ,]_ **[ h]** _[m] w[,]_ **[ h]**[[SEP]] _[}]_[.] Finally the hidden representation of the target region is optimized against negative ones via classification or ranking loss, where new task-specific parameters are introduced. As a result, fine-tuned VL-PTMs need a large mount of labeled instances to stimulate the visual grounding capability.

## 3 CROSS-MODAL PROMPT TUNING (CPT)

In this section, we introduce the framework of CPT, and how to apply CPT to zero-shot, few-shot and fully supervised visual grounding.

## 3.1 OVERVIEW

The key to visual grounding is to establish fine-grained connections between image regions and textual expressions. Therefore, a good cross-modal prompt tuning framework should take full advantage of co-referential signals from both image and text, and maximally mitigate the gap between pre-training and tuning. To this end, CPT reformulates visual grounding into a fill-in-the-blank problem, as shown in Figure 1. Specifically, the CPT framework consists of two components: (1) a _visual sub-prompt_ that uniquely marks the image regions with colored blocks or segmentation masks, and (2) a _textual sub-prompt_ that puts the query text into a color-based query template. Equipped with CPT, it is then straightforward for VL-PTMs to ground the query text by filling the masked token with the color text of the target image region, where the objective form is identical to pre-training.

## 3.2 VISUAL SUB-PROMPT

Given an image _I_ and its region proposals _R_ = _{v_ 1 _, v_ 2 _, . . . , vn}_ , visual sub-prompt aims to uniquely mark the image regions with natural visual makers. Interestingly, we note that colored bounding boxes are widely used to uniquely mark objects in images _for visualization_ in the literature. Inspired by this, we bridge the image regions and query text through a set of colors _C_ , where each color _ci_ = ( _c[i] v[, c][i] w_[)] _[∈C]_[is defined by its visual appearance] _[ c][i] v_[(e.g.,][RGB (][255][,][0][,][0][)) and color text] _[ c][i] w_ (e.g., _red_ ). Then we mark each region proposal _vi_ in the image with a unique color _c[i] v_[for grounding,] resulting in a set of colored image proposals Ψ( _R_ ; _C_ ), where Ψ( _·_ ) denotes visual sub-prompt.

As for the shape of the visual sub-prompt, in principle, there are multiple plausible choices to mark the regions with colors, including colored bounding boxes, solid blocks, or solid object segmentation masks. In our experiments, we find that coloring the object with solid blocks and segmentation masks yields better results than bounding boxes, since solid colors that fit the outlines of objects are more common in real-world images (e.g., _red shirt_ and _blue car_ ). Note that the addition of visual sub-prompt to the raw image does not change the architecture or parameters of VL-PTMs.

## 3.3 TEXTUAL SUB-PROMPT

Textual sub-prompt aims to prompt VL-PTMs to establish the connections between the query text and image regions marked by visual sub-prompt. Specifically, the query text _q_ (e.g., “ _the horse watched by the woman_ ”) is transformed into a fill-in-the-blank query using a template _Tg_ ( _·_ ) as:

## 3.4 TRAINING AND INFERENCE

Equipped with CPT, VL-PTMs can readily perform zero-shot visual grounding without any labeled data, since the cross-modal representations of colors and their composition with other concepts (e.g., objects, attributes and relations) have been well learned by VL-PTMs during pre-training. Although it is appealing to bridge the image and text through a color-based prompt, we identify two key challenges in its design: (1) how to determine the configurations of the color set _C_ , and (2) how to deal with the large number of image regions with limited pre-trained colors.

**Cross-Modal Prompt Search.** Previous works in textual prompt tuning show that prompt configurations (e.g., textual templates) have a significant influence on the performance (Jiang et al., 2020). In this work, we make the first investigation in searching the cross-modal prompt configuration (i.e., the color set _C_ ). Intuitively, _C_ should consist of colors to which VL-PTMs are the most sensitive. To obtain a color _ci_ = ( _c[i] v[, c][i] w_[)][,][a][naive][approach][is][to][adopt][the][most][frequent][color][text][in][the] pre-training text as _c[i] w_[,][and][its][standard][RGB][as] _[c][i] v_[(e.g.,] _[c][i]_[=][((255] _[,]_[ 0] _[,]_[ 0)] _[,][ red]_[)][).][However,][this] solution is sub-optimal, since it determines the color text without considering its visual appearance, and the visual appearance of a color in real-world images often differs from its standard RGB.

To address the challenge, we present a principled cross-modal prompt search (CPS) algorithm for CPT, which jointly considers visual and textual semantics in real-world cross-modal data. Specifically, we first identify a candidate set of color texts _C_[ˆ] _w_ and visual appearances _C_[ˆ] _v_ . For each visual appearance candidate _c_ ˆ _v ∈ C_[ˆ] _v_ , we feed into VL-PTMs a pseudo-data instance consisting of a pure colored block of _c_ ˆ _v_ and a text: “[CLS] a photo in [MASK] color [SEP]”. Then we compute the decoding score _s_ ( ˆ _cv, c_ ˆ _w_ ) for each color text candidate _c_ ˆ _w ∈ C_[ˆ] _w_ as in Equation 1, where a larger decoding score indicates higher correlation between _c_ ˆ _v_ and _c_ ˆ _w_ . To select the color texts that are sensitive by VL-PTMs, we retain the color texts that achieve the largest decoding scores for visual appearance candidates: _Cw_ = _{cw|cw_ = arg max _c_ ˆ _jw[∈][C]_[ˆ] _w[s]_[(ˆ] _[c] v[i][,]_[ ˆ] _[c][j] w_[)] _[,]_[ ˆ] _[c][i] v[∈][C]_[ˆ] _[v][}]_[.][Similarly,][we][can] obtain the visual appearances according to the largest decoding score, resulting in the color set: _C_ = _{_ ( _cv, cw_ ) _|cv_ = arg max _c_ ˆ _iv∈C_ ˆ _v s_ (ˆ _c[i] v[, c][j] w_[)] _[, c][j] w[∈C][w][}]_[.][We][refer][readers][to][Section][B][for][the] pseudo-code of the algorithm. In experiments, we find that the resultant colors yield better results than the naive ones. To make the raw content of the colored image regions available to VL-PTMs, a transparency hyperparameter _α ∈_ (0 _,_ 1) is further applied to color visual appearances in practice.

**Image Region Batching.** In visual grounding, the number of region proposals in an image usually exceeds the size of _C_ ( _∼_ 10). Besides, we observe that heavily overlapped colored blocks can hinder visual grounding. Therefore, we divide the image regions into batches, where each batch contains a handful of moderately overlapping image regions, and mark each batch with a visual sub-prompt respectively. To handle the batches that do not contain the target region, we further introduce a new candidate text _none_ in the decoding vocabulary, to indicate that there is no target region in the batch.

## 3.5 CPT FOR PREDICATE CLASSIFICATION

In the previous sections, we introduced CPT for visual grounding. In fact, CPT can also be easily adapted to other cross-modal tasks, such as predicate classification. Given an object pair (including the categories and bounding boxes) in an image, predicate classification aims to classify the relation into a relation set _P_ , providing structured image representations that can facilitate many cross-modal tasks (Johnson et al., 2015; Hudson & Manning, 2019; Shi et al., 2019). In the literature, since the ground-truth relations cannot be exhaustively annotated during evaluation, to avoid false negatives, previous works typically score the triplets and evaluate the recall of top-N triplets (Xu et al., 2017; Zellers et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2019; Tang et al., 2019).

**Visual and Textual Sub-prompts.** As shown in Figure 2, to perform predicate classification, CPT first marks the image regions with visual sub-prompt as in Section 3.2, and puts the object pair in the query template as follows:

accommodate the varied number of tokens in relation texts (e.g., _wearing_ , _walking on_ , typically 1 _∼_ 3 tokens), we introduce a variable _l_ indicating the number of tokens in a relation text (e.g., _l_ = 2 for _walking on_ ). The template _T_ ( _·_ ; _l_ ) will have _l_ consecutive masked tokens for relation prediction. For each template _T_ ( _·_ ; _l_ ), we introduce a special NA relation consisting of _l_ tokens, which indicates that there is no relation between the entity pair under _T_ ( _·_ ; _l_ ). Specifically, in our experiments, the NA relation is _irrelevant_ , _no relation_ , _no relation with_ for _l_ = 1 _,_ 2 _,_ 3 respectively.

**Training** . Given a relational triplet ( _s, r, o_ ), after decorating the input image regions and the object pair with visual and textual sub-prompts, VL-PTMs are optimized with the MLM loss to recover the relational tokens. Specifically, denote the number of tokens in _r_ as _|r|_ . (1) For templates where _l_ = _|r|_ , models are asked to reconstruct the _i_ th masked token in _T_ ( _s, o_ ; _l_ ) with the _i_ th relational token _ri_ using the MLM head. (2) For templates where _l_ = _|r|_ , since there is no relation between ( _s, o_ ) under _T_ ( _s, o_ ; _l_ ), models are asked to reconstruct the NA relation. For ( _s, o_ ) that do not have any relation in the image, models are asked to reconstruct the NA relation for all _T_ ( _s, o_ ; _l_ ).

**Inference** . During inference, given an object pair ( _s, o_ ), we score the relations based on their fitness to the prompt context. Specifically, the score of each relation _r ∈P ∪{_ NA _}_ is obtained by the aggregated MLM scores of its composing tokens under the corresponding template: _l s_ ( _r_ ) =[1] _l_ � _i_ =1[log] _[ P]_[(][[MASK]] _[i]_[=] _[r][i][|T]_[ (] _[s, o]_[;] _[ l]_[))][,][where] _[l]_[=] _[|][r][|]_[.][Intuitively,][larger] _[s]_[(] _[r]_[)][indicates] that the relation _r_ better fits the prompt context. Finally, the triplets ( _s, r, o_ ) are ranked according to the relation score _s_ ( _r_ ), where _r ∈P_ .

Compared with visual grounding that aims to locate image regions for ungrounded texts, predicate classification represents a different series of cross-modal tasks that aim to perform semantic recognition based on grounded inputs, such as object classification (Zhao et al., 2017) and scene graph classification (Xu et al., 2017). In addition to better data efficiency, a crucial advantage of using CPT is that the semantic labels can be produced from open-world vocabularies, instead of fixed label sets.

## 4 EXPERIMENTS

In this section, we empirically evaluate CPT in prompting VL-PTMs for visual grounding in different settings, including zero-shot, few-shot and fully supervised settings. We refer readers to Section C for the implementation details.

## 4.1 EXPERIMENTAL SETTINGS

We first introduce the experimental settings of the visual grounding task, including datasets, training settings, evaluation protocols and baseline models in our experiments.

**Datasets.** Following previous works (Rohrbach et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2018), we adopt three widely used visual grounding datasets collected from MSCOCO images (Lin et al., 2014), including RefCOCO (Yu et al., 2016), RefCOCO+ (Yu et al., 2016) and RefCOCOg (Mao et al., 2016). We refer readers to Section D.2 for more dataset details.

**Training Settings.** We report experimental results of different training settings, including (1) zeroshot setting, where no training data is available, (2) few-shot setting, where _K_ training instances are available ( _K_ = 1 _,_ 2 _,_ 4 _,_ 8 _,_ 16), and (3) fully supervised setting, where the full training set is available.

**Evaluation Protocols.** (1) Evaluation metrics. Following Zhang et al. (2018); Lu et al. (2019), we adopt accuracy of the grounding results as the evaluation metrics. An expression is considered correctly grounded if the IoU of the top predicted region and the ground truth is greater than 0 _._ 5. (2) Model validation. To better approximate the few-shot scenario where only a few labeled instances are available, inspired by Gao et al. (2021), we use a few-shot validation set (consisting of 16 instances) for few-shot and zero-shot experiments, and use full validation set for fully supervised experiments. (3) Robust evaluation. Previous works have shown that model training on limited data can suffer from instability (Dodge et al., 2020; Gao et al., 2021). For a robust and comprehensive evaluation, we report mean results over 5 random training set splits, as well as the standard deviation. For fair comparisons, the training and validation sets are identical for our baselines and CPT.
**Baselines.** We evaluate two variants of CPT, including CPT using colored blocks (CPT-Blk) and colored segmentation masks (CPT-Seg). We adopt the widely used VinVL (Zhang et al., 2021)

as the CPT backbone. We compare CPT with a series of strong baselines that utilize detected proposals, including vanilla fine-tuning of VinVL and other VL-PTMs (see Section D.1 for more baseline details). For fair comparisons, we adopt the base size for all VL-PTMs. We refer readers to Section A.1 for the results of large size VL-PTMs.

## 4.2 MAIN RESULTS

The main results are reported in Table 1, from which we observe that: (1) CPT outperforms the random baseline and the strong fine-tuning baseline by a large margin in zero-shot and few-shot settings. For example, using colored blocks as visual sub-prompts, CPT achieves 17 _._ 3% absolute accuracy improvement on average with one shot in RefCOCO evaluation. This indicates that CPT can effectively improve sample efficiency in tuning VL-PTMs. (2) Coloring objects with segmentation masks in visual sub-prompts (CPT-Seg) achieves even better results than blocks (CPT-Blk). The reason is that solid colors that fit the outlines of objects are more common in real-world images, making CPT-Seg more natural visual sub-prompts (despite requiring stronger annotation to train the segmentation tools). (3) Notably, CPT achieves significantly smaller standard deviation than fine-tuning. For example, CPT-Blk achieves 73 _._ 8% relative standard deviation reduction on average with one shot in RefCOCO evaluation. This shows that a coherent tuning approach from pre-training can lead to substantially more stable few-shot training, which is a crucial factor for evaluating few-shot learning models (Gao et al., 2021). (4) We note that CPT-Blk slightly underperforms fine-tuning with 16 shots in RefCOCO+ evaluation. The reason is that RefCOCO+ has more colorbased expressions (e.g., _the person in red shirt and blue hat_ ), which can disturb our color-based CPT. However, this problem can be alleviated with more tuning instances in the fully supervised scenario, where models can learn to better distinguish colors in the query text and prompt template. (5) CPT models achieve comparable performance to strong fine-tuned VL-PTMs in the fully supervised settings. This shows that CPT is a competitive tuning approach for VL-PTMs even in the fully supervised scenario. We note that CPT-Blk slightly outperforms CPT-Seg in the fully supervised setting, and we refer readers to Section A.3 for a detailed analysis. In summary, compared to the vanilla fine-tuning approach, CPT achieves superior/comparable, and more stable performance in zero-shot, few-shot and fully supervised visual grounding.

## 4.3 INFLUENCE OF COLORS IN CPT’S VISUAL GROUNDING

In our analysis, we first investigate the influence of colors—the key ingredients—in the visual grounding performance of CPT. Specifically, we compare colors obtained from the frequency-based baseline ( **Freq** ) (See Section 3.4) and our cross-modal prompt search method CPS ( **Ours** ) in two dimensions, including an overall evaluation of top-N colors and a zoom-in study of individual colors. Unless otherwise specified, all the following experiments are conducted based on CPT-Blk on the validation set of RefCOCO in 0 _,_ 2 _,_ 8 shot settings.
Table 2: Top-6 colors from the frequency-based baseline and our CPS. Visual appearances and color texts are reported. Best viewed in color.

**Overall Evaluation of Top-N Colors.** We first show the top-6 colors recommended by each approach in Table 2. To evaluate the overall performance of the top colors from different models, we evaluate CPT equipped with each color from the top-6 colors respectively, and report the mean accuracy and standard deviation over different colors. From the experimental results in Figure 3a, we observe that the top colors produced by CPS achieve both higher mean accuracy and lower standard deviation than the baseline method in different shot-settings. The reason is that CPS jointly considers visual and textual semantics in searching cross-modal prompts, and therefore is able to effectively adjust and rank the colors for more accurate and stable visual grounding.

**Zoom-In Study of Individual Colors.** To investigate the fine-grained influence of specific colors in CPT’s visual grounding, we further perform a zoom-in study of individual colors. To align the colors for comparison, we merge the top-6 colors from the baseline and CPS, and remove the colors that are not included in the models’ complete color sets (e.g., _black ∈C/_ in CPS). We report the accuracies in Figure 3b, from which we observe that: (1) The performance of different colors varies greatly in prompting VL-PTMs in the same shot-settings, and the optimal colors are different in different shotsettings. The results indicate the large influence of cross-modal prompt configurations, consistent with the findings from recent studies in textual prompt tuning (Jiang et al., 2020; Gao et al., 2021). (2) Colors produced by CPS achieve comparable or superior performance compared to the baseline in individual colors. The results show that given the color texts, CPS can properly adjust the color visual appearance (i.e., RGB) to improve the visual grounding performance. (3) We note that in some cases, colors produced by CPS slightly underperform the baseline. We hypothesize the reason is that, CPS uses a single textual template to compute the decoding scores for color adjustment, which can be biased. The problem can potentially be addressed by ensembling templates as in Qin & Eisner (2021), which we leave for future work.

(2) CPT can be distracted by hard candidates (e.g., objects of the same type as the target that requires complex reasoning to identify), but will typically produce reasonable predictions. For example, in Figure 4b, CPT predicts a nearby _apple_ while the fine-tuning baseline predicts a _bowl_ . The reason is that CPT maximally reuses the pre-trained parameters of VL-PTMs, which can help prevent outrageous predictions that typically happen in few-shot fine-tuning. (3) However, we find that CPT can be disturbed by colors in raw image regions and text. For example, it can be difficult for the model to identify a _red bowl_ when the candidate regions are colored by red blocks (Figure 4c).

## 4.5 EXPERIMENTS ON PREDICATE CLASSIFICATION

To investigate the generalization capability of CPT, we evaluate CPT on predicate classification task.

**Experimental Settings.** (1) Datasets. We adopt the popular Visual Genome dataset (Krishna et al., 2017), which contains 50 visual relations. We refer readers to Section D.2 for the dataset details. (2) Evaluation protocols. Following previous works (Xu et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2019), we use recall@N (R@N) and mean recall@N (mR@N) as the evaluation metrics. During training, K labeled instances are provided for each relation. (3) Baselines. We adopt fine-tuning of VinVL as our most direct baseline model. Specifically, we feed the image regions and their categories into the model, and concatenate the visual hidden representations of the subject and object. Then the object pair representation is fed into a softmax classifier. All VL-PTMs are in base size. We also report the results of strong baselines that are tailored for the task, and are fully supervised with 315 _,_ 642 labeled triplets, including Neural Motif (Zellers et al., 2018), BGNN (Li et al., 2021a), PCPL (Yan et al., 2020) and DT2-ACBS (Desai et al., 2021).

8

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

**Results.** From the results in Table 3, we observe that: (1) CPT significantly outperforms the random baseline and the strong fine-tuning baseline in zero-shot and few-shot settings. For example, using 32 shots, CPT achieves a strong mR@100 of 37 _._ 7%, outperforming fine-tuning by 13 _._ 6% absolute points, and closely approaching state-of-the-art fully supervised DT2-ACBS. This indicates that CPT can improve sample efficiency in tuning VL-PTMs. (2) We note that while the macro performance of CPT monotonically increases as the shot number grows, the micro performance drops first in 1- and 4-shot settings. This is due to the distribution gap between the balanced training set (i.e., K shot for each relation) and the long-tail test set. Since the relations in the pre-training corpora also follow a long-tail distribution, CPT can achieve a high starting point for micro performance.

## 5 RELATED WORK

**Pre-trained Vision-language Models.** Existing VL-PTMs can be roughly divided into three categories according to their pre-training objectives and architectures: (1) _Masked language modeling_ based VL-PTMs are mainly pre-trained to recover the masked tokens (Lu et al., 2019; Su et al., 2019; Tan & Bansal, 2019; Li et al., 2020; Yu et al., 2021); (2) _Auto-regressive language modeling_ based VL-PTMs model image and text tokens with Transformer decoders auto-regressively (Ramesh et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2021); (3) _Contrastive learning_ based VL-PTMs are pre-trained to holistically match image-text pairs (Radford et al., 2021; Li et al., 2021b). Note that our Cross-modal Prompt Tuning (CPT) framework is orthogonal to VL-PTM design. In this work, without loss of generality, we focus on prompting masked language modeling based VL-PTMs due to their prevalence and superior performance, while applying CPT to other VL-PTMs is also applicable.

**Prompt Tuning for NLP.** Prompt tuning for pre-trained language models is a rapidly emerging field in NLP (Raffel et al., 2019; Brown et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2021). Originally designed for probing knowledge in pre-trained language models (Petroni et al., 2019), prompt tuning has now been extended to handle a variety of NLP tasks, including language understanding (Schick & Sch¨utze, 2021a;b) and generation (Li & Liang, 2021). To facilitate prompt engineering, Shin et al. (2020) propose to automatically generate prompt templates via gradient-based search. Most related to our work are Tsimpoukelli et al. (2021); Zhou et al. (2021); Wang et al. (2021) that present textual prompt tuning for VL-PTMs, achieving promising results on some vision-language tasks. However, similar to existing works in NLP, they focus on prompt engineering in text, keeping images untouched, and therefore can only perform holistic implicit visual grounding. In comparison, to the best of our knowledge, CPT is the first cross-modal prompt tuning framework tailored for both image and text, and is capable of explicitly grounding natural language to fine-grained image regions.

**Visual Grounding.** There is a general consensus that visual grounding plays an essential role in solving vision-language tasks (Karpathy & Fei-Fei, 2015; Plummer et al., 2015; Goodfellow et al., 2016; Krishna et al., 2017; Lu et al., 2019). Mao et al. (2016) propose the referring expression comprehension task to explicitly evaluate the visual grounding capability. To address the task, most models learn to classify or rank image region candidates based on the expressions in a fully supervised fashion (Mao et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2018; Lu et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2020), requiring large amounts of costly human-annotated data. To alleviate reliance on human annotation, some works have investigated zero-/few-shot grounding of new object types (Sadhu et al., 2019; Blukis et al., 2020), whereas amounts of training data are still needed for existing object types. In comparison, we prompt general VL-PTMs for zero- and few-shot visual grounding in a reformulated fill-in-the-blank paradigm independent of specific object types.

## 6 CONCLUSION AND FUTURE WORK

In this work, we present the first Cross-modal Prompt Tuning (CPT) framework for VL-PTMs. To facilitate prompt engineering, we present a principled approach to search for cross-modal prompt configurations. Comprehensive experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of CPT on zeroshot, few-shot and fully supervised visual grounding. In future, we plan to address the color disturbance and improve the computation efficiency of CPT, and also investigate the effectiveness of CPT on other vision-language tasks. As the first attempt in cross-modal prompt tuning, we propose a color-based framework as one of the possible prompt tuning solutions. We leave exploring other plausible prompt tuning approaches of VL-PTMs for future work.

9

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## 7 ETHICS STATEMENT

In this section, we discuss the main ethical considerations of CPT: (1) Intellectual property protection. The codes and data adopted from previous works are granted for research-purpose usage. (2) Privacy. The data adopted in this work (i.e., the pre-training data and tuning data) is created by human annotators for research purposes, and should not cause privacy issues. (3) Potential problems. VL-PTMs may be biased towards some objects and attributes. There are increasing efforts to address the problem in the community (Ross et al., 2021; Zhao et al., 2021).

## 8 REPRODUCIBILITY STATEMENT

To maximize the reproducibility, we provide a clear description of the methodology in Section 3, the pseudo-code of the model in Section B, implementation details in Section C, and detailed data characteristics and evaluation protocols in Section 4.1. All the data and codes will be available to facilitate future research.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes a novel and conceptually interesting approach—Colorful Prompt Tuning (CPT)—that bridges visual and textual modalities via color-based co-referential markers. The idea of reformulating visual grounding as a fill-in-the-blank task using the pre-trained MLM head is well-motivated and aligns with recent trends in prompt-based adaptation. The empirical gains in few-shot and zero-shot settings are substantial and suggest real promise for parameter-efficient tuning in vision-language models. The method is clearly described, and the ablation on color selection (CPS) adds depth.

Overview issues:

- Lack of ablation isolating visual and textual sub-prompts: The core claim of CPT is that both visual and textual sub-prompts work together to improve grounding by reformulating the task into a fill-in-the-blank problem. However, the paper does not include an ablation study that isolates the contribution of each component. For instance, there is no evaluation of a variant that uses only textual prompts with color templates but no visual coloring, or one that applies visual coloring without structured textual templates. Without such controls, it is unclear whether the performance gains stem from the cross-modal alignment or simply from either the visual augmentation or the prompt template alone. This undermines the validity of the central claim that CPT enables cross-modal prompt tuning. The authors should report results for these ablated variants to clarify the source of improvement.

- Ambiguity in how visual sub-prompts are integrated into the image: The paper states that visual sub-prompts are added as colored blocks or segmentation masks but does not specify how these are encoded in the input to the vision encoder. Are they added as pixel-level overlays before patch embedding? Are they inserted as additional tokens in the visual sequence? This ambiguity is critical because different integration strategies can drastically affect how the model processes the prompts. For example, pixel-level overlays may interfere with object semantics, while token-level insertion could bypass low-level feature disruption but require architectural changes. The lack of clarity makes the method difficult to reproduce and raises questions about whether the approach truly preserves the frozen backbone as claimed.

- Insufficient comparison with existing visual prompt tuning methods: The paper positions CPT as a novel cross-modal prompt tuning method but fails to compare against existing visual prompt tuning approaches such as VPT (Visual Prompt Tuning) or MaPLe, which also introduce learnable visual tokens. While CPT uses fixed color embeddings rather than learned ones, the absence of even a qualitative or conceptual comparison leaves readers unable to assess whether the gains are due to the color-based design or simply the general idea of augmenting visual input. A direct comparison with VPT—either by applying it to the same task or citing expected performance differences—would strengthen the paper’s contribution claim.

- Potential data leakage in predicate classification due to color-text overlap: In Section 4.5, CPT is applied to predicate classification using color-based prompts. However, the model is trained to predict relations like 'wearing' or 'on' in the context of color-marked objects. Since many real-world relations (e.g., 'red shirt') involve color, and the model is now being prompted with artificial colors, there is a risk that the model confuses natural color references in the text with the artificial prompt colors. For example, a query like 'the man in red shirt' paired with a red-marked bowl could mislead the model. The paper acknowledges color disturbance in Figure 4c but does not quantify or control for this effect in the predicate task. This raises concerns about the validity of the reported gains, especially in zero-shot settings where such interference may dominate.

- Overstated zero-shot claims due to pre-training on color-related concepts: The paper claims strong zero-shot performance, but this relies on the assumption that the pre-trained model already understands the mapping between color names and visual appearances. However, since models like VinVL were trained on image-caption data containing frequent color-object co-occurrences (e.g., 'red car'), the success of CPT in zero-shot may reflect memorization of these patterns rather than genuine generalization. The zero-shot results could be inflated if the test queries contain color terms that align with common pre-training examples. The authors should evaluate on a subset of queries that avoid color terms or conduct a control experiment where color prompts are mismatched to test robustness.

- Lack of qualitative analysis showing improved visual-text alignment: While the paper includes a case study in Figure 4, it does not provide attention visualizations or other interpretability tools to demonstrate that CPT actually improves visual-text alignment. For example, showing that the [MASK] token attends more strongly to the correctly colored region would provide direct evidence for the mechanism. Without such analysis, the claim that CPT enhances grounding remains speculative. Given that attention patterns are standard in vision-language papers, their absence weakens the paper’s impact and limits insight into how the method works.

- No ablation isolating visual vs textual sub-prompts: The paper claims that CPT's strength comes from cross-modal alignment via color co-reference, but provides no ablation that tests whether both visual and textual components are necessary. A critical missing experiment is evaluating a version of CPT with only the textual prompt (e.g., 'in [MASK] color' template) applied to unmodified images, and another with colored blocks but a flat query (e.g., 'the horse is in the region'). Without these controls, it is impossible to determine whether gains arise from the visual augmentation, the prompt structure, or their interaction. To validate the cross-modal claim, the authors should report results for these variants on RefCOCO in few-shot settings and compare their performance to full CPT.

- Missing comparison to visual prompt tuning baselines: The paper introduces visual sub-prompts but does not compare against existing visual prompting methods like VPT (Visual Prompt Tuning) or MaPLe, which also insert learnable tokens into the visual stream. While CPT uses fixed color embeddings rather than learned ones, the absence of even a conceptual or empirical comparison leaves unclear whether the benefits are due to the color-based design or simply the act of visual augmentation. To properly position CPT, the authors should either run VPT on the same visual grounding task or provide a detailed discussion explaining why color-based fixed prompts are preferable to learned visual prompts in terms of data efficiency, stability, or cross-modal alignment.

- No analysis of attention patterns for visual grounding: The paper claims that CPT improves fine-grained visual-text alignment but offers no direct evidence from model internals. Readers will want to see whether the [MASK] token attends more strongly to the correctly colored region than to others, or whether attention becomes sharper under CPT compared to fine-tuning. This can be shown via attention heatmaps over the image patches for different color-marked regions. Such analysis is standard in vision-language papers and would strengthen the mechanistic claim. The authors should include attention visualizations for 2-3 examples from RefCOCO, highlighting how CPT directs focus to the target region via color co-reference.

- Zero-shot results may reflect color memorization: The strong zero-shot performance assumes that color grounding generalizes, but may instead reflect memorization of frequent color-object pairs from pre-training (e.g., 'red car'). A more rigorous test would evaluate on a subset of queries that avoid explicit color terms in the text, or use color prompts that mismatch common associations (e.g., marking a car as green when 'green car' is rare in pre-training data). Without such a control, the zero-shot claim is questionable. The authors should report zero-shot accuracy on color-free queries or conduct a perturbation experiment where prompt colors are systematically rotated to assess robustness to unnatural mappings.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add ablation studies that isolate the contribution of visual and textual sub-prompts (e.g., text-only color prompts, visual-only coloring) to validate the cross-modal design.

- Clarify how visual sub-prompts are integrated into the image input—specifically, whether they are pixel-level overlays or token-level additions—and confirm that no architectural changes are made to the frozen vision encoder.

- Include comparisons with existing visual prompt tuning methods like VPT or MaPLe, either through direct experimentation or detailed discussion of architectural and functional differences.

- Address potential data leakage in predicate classification by analyzing performance on color-free queries or reporting results with shuffled color assignments.

- Provide attention visualizations or other interpretability analyses to demonstrate that CPT improves visual-text alignment as claimed.

comments.title: Incorrect claim of being first cross-modal prompt tuning framework
comments.quote: In this work, we present the first Cross-modal Prompt Tuning (CPT) framework for VL-PTMs.
comments.explanation: The claim that CPT is the 'first' cross-modal prompt tuning framework contradicts established work. Methods such as MaPLe (Multi-Modal Prompt Learning) and VPT (Visual Prompt Tuning) already introduce learnable visual prompts in vision-language models, which constitute a form of cross-modal prompting when combined with textual inputs. While CPT may be the first to use color-based visual sub-prompts, the broader category of cross-modal prompt tuning has prior instances. The paper does not provide a comparative discussion to justify the 'first' designation. To correct this, the authors should either qualify the claim or cite and contrast with existing visual prompting methods to establish novelty.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of prior visual prompting methods
comments.quote: However, similar to existing works in NLP, they focus on prompt engineering in text, keeping images untouched, and therefore can only perform holistic implicit visual grounding.
comments.explanation: This statement inaccurately dismisses existing visual prompt tuning methods such as VPT, which explicitly modifies the image input by injecting learnable visual tokens into the vision encoder. VPT does not 'keep images untouched' and is designed precisely to enable finer-grained visual interactions. By overlooking VPT and similar works, the paper creates a false contrast between textual-only prompting and CPT’s supposedly novel visual-textual co-design. The authors should revise the claim to reflect that visual prompting already exists, and position CPT as a color-based variant rather than a fundamentally new direction.

comments.title: Inconsistent handling of transparency in visual prompts
comments.quote: To make the raw content of the colored image regions available to VL-PTMs, a transparency hyperparameter  $\alpha \in (0, 1)$  is further applied to color visual appearances in practice.
comments.explanation: The introduction of $\alpha$ is presented as a practical choice, but its interaction with the CPS algorithm is unexplained. The CPS uses pure colored blocks (fully opaque) to estimate decoding scores, yet in training, colored regions are semi-transparent. This creates a distribution shift: the visual appearance used during search differs from that used during deployment. As a result, the selected color set $\mathcal{C}$ may not be optimal under transparency. The method should either re-run CPS with $\alpha$-blended colors or justify why opacity does not affect color sensitivity.

comments.title: Mismatched color texts in predicate classification template
comments.quote: The  $s_w$  in  $c_w^i$  color is [MASK] the  $o_w$  in  $c_w^i$  color [SEP]
comments.explanation: The definition explicitly introduces two distinct color texts $c_w^i$ and $c_w^j$ for the subject and object, respectively. However, the template uses $c_w^i$ for both the subject and object regions. This creates a binding error: if the subject is red and the object is blue, the model sees 'the red object is [MASK] the red object', which misaligns the textual description with the visual grounding. Since the visual sub-prompts use distinct colors to disambiguate regions, the textual template must reflect this distinction to maintain cross-modal coherence. Rewrite the template to use distinct color variables for subject and object.

comments.title: NA relation token count mismatch risk
comments.quote: Specifically, in our experiments, the NA relation is *irrelevant*, *no relation*, *no relation with* for $l=1,2,3$ respectively.
comments.explanation: The NA phrases are assigned based on $l$, but their actual tokenization length depends on the tokenizer. For example, 'no relation' may tokenize as ['no', 'relation'] (2 tokens) in WordPiece, but 'relation' might be split into ['rel', '##ation'], making it 3 tokens instead of 2. This breaks the assumption that the NA phrase exactly fills $l$ masked positions. During training, the model expects to reconstruct $l$ tokens, so a mismatch leads to label misalignment and degraded learning. Add a tokenizer-aware definition of NA relations, e.g., using fixed multi-mask tokens like [NA1], [NA2], [NA3] mapped to $l=1,2,3$, to ensure consistent length.

comments.title: Ranking excludes NA despite scoring it
comments.quote: Finally, the triplets $(s,r,o)$ are ranked according to the relation score $s(r)$, where $r\in\mathcal{P}$.
comments.explanation: The scoring function $s(r)$ is defined over $r \in \mathcal{P} \cup \{\texttt{NA}\}$, indicating that NA is a valid output with a computable likelihood. However, the ranking procedure only considers $r \in \mathcal{P}$, effectively ignoring the possibility that NA is the best-fitting relation. This creates a decision-theoretic inconsistency: if the model assigns higher probability to NA than any semantic relation, it should abstain from predicting a relation, but the current setup forces a choice from $\mathcal{P}$. This undermines the purpose of training with NA as a negative class and may increase false positives. Revise the ranking to include $r \in \mathcal{P} \cup \{\texttt{NA}\}$ and only form triplets when the top-scoring relation is not NA.

comments.title: No handling of overlapping relation lengths
comments.quote: modate the varied number of tokens in relation texts (e.g., *wearing*, *walking on*, typically 1$\sim$3 tokens), we introduce a variable $l$ indicating the number of tokens in a relation text (e.g., $l=2$ for *walking on*).
comments.explanation: The method defines separate templates for each $l=1,2,3$, but does not specify how conflicting predictions across $l$ are resolved. For example, the same object pair might yield a high score for 'on' ($l=1$) and 'walking on' ($l=2$). Since each template operates independently, there is no mechanism to compare or calibrate scores across different $l$. The final ranking uses $s(r)$ without normalizing for template length or masking structure, which may favor shorter relations due to higher per-token confidence. Add a score normalization scheme or a joint decoding mechanism across $l$ to resolve length-based conflicts.

comments.title: Training loss does not account for hierarchical relation semantics
comments.quote: (1) For templates where $l=|r|$, models are asked to reconstruct the $i$th masked token in $\mathcal{T}(s,o;l)$ with the $i$th relational token $r_{i}$ using the MLM head. (2) For templates where $l\neq|r|$, since there is no relation between $(s,o)$ under $\mathcal{T}(s,o;l)$, models are asked to reconstruct the NA relation.
comments.explanation: The training procedure treats each template $\mathcal{T}(\cdot;l)$ independently and assigns NA to all $l \neq |r|$. However, a relation like 'walking on' ($|r|=2$) may semantically entail 'on' ($l=1$), so labeling the $l=1$ template with NA is incorrect. The model is penalized for predicting 'on' in the $l=1$ template even if it is a valid sub-relation. This creates conflicting supervision signals across templates. The assumption that only one $l$ can be valid is too strong and ignores hierarchical or compositional semantics in relations. Revise the training loss to allow multiple active templates per relation or define a hierarchical label structure to avoid penalizing semantically consistent predictions.

comments.title: Missing standard deviation in fully supervised results
comments.explanation: The fully supervised results report single values without standard deviations, unlike the few-shot and zero-shot settings, which include mean ± std over 5 splits. This is inconsistent with the stated evaluation protocol: 'we report mean results over 5 random training set splits, as well as the standard deviation'. Since the fully supervised setting also uses the same training data, it should reflect variability across splits. Either report the standard deviation for these models or clarify that full supervision uses a single fixed split. The omission breaks the paper’s own robustness claim.

comments.title: Inconsistent table formatting breaks data alignment
comments.quote: |  Shot | Model | RefCOCO |   |   | RefCOCO+ |   |   | RefCOCOg  |   |   |
comments.explanation: The table uses inconsistent column grouping: 'RefCOCO', 'RefCOCO+', and 'RefCOCOg' each span three columns (val, testA, testB), but the header row shows extra blank columns, leading to misalignment. Specifically, the third row under 'RefCOCOg' has 'val | test  |   |' — the second and third entries are 'test' and blank instead of 'testA' and 'testB'. This formatting error causes confusion in reading results, especially since the last column for RefCOCOg is left empty in several rows. Rewrite the table with consistent column headers and complete entries to ensure accurate interpretation.

comments.title: CPT-Seg entry misplaced in final row
comments.explanation: The row starting with '|  CPT-Seg |   |' is incorrectly formatted — the model name appears in the Shot column and the Model column is empty, breaking the table structure. This row corresponds to the K=16 few-shot setting, as seen from the preceding rows, but is detached from the grouped layout. It should be aligned under the 'Few-Shot' and '16' hierarchy like previous entries. The misplacement makes it hard to determine which setting this result belongs to and risks misinterpretation. Move this row into the correct hierarchical block and align it with the 'Model' column to maintain consistency.

comments.title: Baseline model name typo: MAtNet instead of MATNet
comments.explanation: The baseline 'MAtNet' is likely a typo for 'MATNet' (Multi-modal Attention Network), a known model in vision-language grounding. The capitalization pattern 'MAtNet' (with lowercase 'A') is inconsistent with standard naming conventions and may confuse readers. Given that other models like UNITER and VL-T5 are correctly capitalized, this appears to be an OCR or formatting error. Correct 'MAtNet' to 'MATNet' to align with established literature and avoid misrepresentation.

comments.title: Grammatical error obscures technical meaning
comments.quote: CPT enables VL-PTMs to distinguish target objects distracted by the same of type objects using
comments.explanation: The phrase 'the same of type objects' is grammatically incorrect and impedes comprehension. It likely intends to say 'objects of the same type' or 'same-type objects'. As written, it creates ambiguity about whether the model handles distractions from co-category instances. This is a critical concept in referring expression comprehension, so clarity is essential. Rewrite as 'distracted by objects of the same type' to accurately convey the challenge of distinguishing between semantically similar instances.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
The paper presents CPT (Colorful Prompt Tuning), a novel approach for adapting pre-trained vision-language models to visual grounding tasks using color-based co-referential markers. While the method shows impressive few-shot results, several critical issues need to be addressed regarding the robustness of the color-based approach, generalization capabilities, and completeness of the evaluation.

The paper introduces an innovative cross-modal prompt tuning framework that cleverly reformulates visual grounding as a fill-in-the-blank problem. The color-based approach is creative and the few-shot results are striking (17.3% absolute improvement with 1-shot). However, the method's reliance on color markers raises fundamental questions about robustness and generalization that are not sufficiently addressed in the current version.

Overview issues:

- Color Ambiguity and Real-World Robustness: The core mechanism of CPT relies on color-based markers, but the paper does not adequately address how the method handles color ambiguity in natural images. Real-world scenes frequently contain objects with similar colors, partial occlusions, and varying lighting conditions that could confuse the color-based grounding. The case study in Figure 4c shows that CPT can be "disturbed by colors in raw image regions and text," but this limitation is only acknowledged in passing. The paper should include systematic experiments evaluating CPT's performance on images with: (1) multiple objects of the same color, (2) color variations due to lighting/shadows, and (3) partially occluded colored objects. Without this analysis, it's unclear whether the impressive few-shot results will hold in more challenging real-world scenarios.

- Limited Evaluation on Color-Heavy Datasets: The paper acknowledges that RefCOCO+ "has more color-based expressions" which "can disturb our color-based CPT," yet only reports that this problem "can be alleviated with more tuning instances." This is insufficient - the authors should report detailed results on RefCOCO+ showing exactly how much performance degrades and analyze failure cases. More importantly, the paper lacks evaluation on datasets specifically designed to test color-based reasoning (e.g., datasets with color-focused referring expressions). Without such evaluation, the claim that CPT achieves "strong few-shot and even zero-shot visual grounding capabilities" appears overstated for scenarios involving color-rich queries.

- Computational Overhead of Color Processing: The paper claims that adding visual sub-prompts "does not change the architecture or parameters of VL-PTMs" but does not address the computational cost of generating and processing colored regions. The image region batching approach (Section 3.4) suggests additional processing steps compared to standard fine-tuning. The paper should report: (1) the additional time required to apply colored blocks/masks to region proposals, (2) the impact of batching on inference speed, and (3) memory overhead from processing multiple batches per image. Without these metrics, readers cannot assess the practical feasibility of CPT for real-time applications.

- Inadequate Ablation of Color-Based Components: While the paper compares CPT-Blk vs CPT-Seg and includes color search analysis, it lacks a critical ablation: what happens without the color-based reformulation? The paper should include a baseline that uses standard prompt tuning (text-only prompts) on the same few-shot visual grounding task to isolate the contribution of the color-based approach. Additionally, the cross-modal prompt search (CPS) method needs deeper analysis - the paper notes it can be "biased" due to using a single template but doesn't quantify this bias or compare against alternative search strategies. Without these ablations, it's difficult to determine whether the gains come from the color-based reformulation itself or from better prompt engineering in general.

- Unexplored Generalization to Other VLM Architectures: The paper focuses exclusively on masked language modeling based VL-PTMs (specifically VinVL) and states that applying CPT to other VL-PTMs is "applicable" without providing any evidence. Given the fundamental differences between masked LM, auto-regressive, and contrastive VLMs, this claim needs verification. The paper should include experiments on at least one contrasting architecture (e.g., CLIP) to demonstrate that the color-based approach transfers across different pre-training objectives. Without this, the contribution appears limited to a specific subset of VLMs.

- Missing zero-shot demonstration failure cases: The paper claims CPT enables zero-shot visual grounding but only shows success metrics. To demonstrate the method's limitations and boundaries, the paper should include specific failure cases where the zero-shot approach fails. For instance, show examples where the model incorrectly grounds expressions involving color adjectives (like 'red car') when the colored blocks interfere with the object's natural color. The authors should analyze at least 5-10 systematic failure modes in zero-shot setting and discuss whether these are fundamental limitations or could be addressed with minimal adaptation.

- No worked parametric example: Despite presenting a principled cross-modal prompt search algorithm, the paper never walks through a complete concrete example of how colors are selected. The authors should compute the decoding scores for a small set of candidate colors (e.g., red, blue, green) showing both the visual appearances and resulting scores from the pseudo-data instance. This would help readers understand why certain RGB values are chosen over standard colors and validate that the search algorithm actually finds better color configurations than naive frequency-based selection.

- Unresolved color disturbance in queries: The paper acknowledges that color-based expressions in queries can disturb CPT (Section 4.2, point 4) but never provides a solution or systematic analysis. The authors should quantify this effect by evaluating CPT on a curated subset of RefCOCO+ containing color-based queries, comparing performance against queries without color references. More importantly, they should propose and evaluate at least one mitigation strategy, such as color word masking or alternative prompt templates for color-heavy queries.

- Missing inference implementation details: The paper describes the image region batching approach but provides no practical guidance for implementation. Readers need to know: (1) the batching algorithm for selecting which regions to group together, (2) how the 'none' candidate is integrated into the softmax calculation across batches, and (3) the computational complexity analysis of processing multiple batches per image. Without these details, reproducing the inference procedure is difficult, and the claim of practical applicability remains unsubstantiated.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add systematic robustness experiments evaluating CPT on images with color ambiguity, similar-colored objects, and occlusions

- Include detailed analysis and results on RefCOCO+ showing how color-based expressions affect performance

- Report computational costs including processing time, inference speed, and memory overhead

- Add ablation study comparing against text-only prompt tuning to isolate the color-based contribution

- Demonstrate CPT on at least one contrasting VLM architecture (e.g., CLIP) to verify generalization claims

comments.title: Mathematical error in color set definition
comments.quote: Similarly, we can obtain the visual appearances according to the largest decoding score, resulting in the color set:  $\mathcal{C} = \{(c_v, c_w) | c_v = \arg \max_{\hat{c}_v^i \in \hat{c}_w} s(\hat{c}_v^i, c_w^i), c_w^i \in \mathcal{C}_w\}$ .
comments.explanation: There is a mathematical error in the set notation. The argmax condition uses $\hat{c}_v^i \in \hat{c}_w$ which is incorrect - visual appearance candidates $\hat{c}_v^i$ should belong to the visual appearance candidate set $\hat{c}_v$, not the text candidate set $\hat{c}_w$. This creates a type mismatch. The correct expression should be $c_v = \arg \max_{\hat{c}_v^i \in \hat{c}_v} s(\hat{c}_v^i, c_w^i)$.

comments.title: Missing decoding score equation reference
comments.quote: Then we compute the decoding score  $s(\hat{c}_v, \hat{c}_w)$  for each color text candidate  $\hat{c}_w \in \hat{c}_w$  as in Equation 1, where a larger decoding score indicates higher correlation between  $\hat{c}_v$  and  $\hat{c}_w$ .
comments.explanation: The text references 'Equation 1' for computing the decoding score, but this equation is not provided in the current section. Without the actual mathematical definition of the decoding score function s(·,·), readers cannot understand how the correlation between visual and textual color candidates is quantified. This is critical since the entire cross-modal prompt search algorithm depends on this scoring mechanism.

comments.title: Training logic contradiction in predicate classification
comments.quote: (1) For templates where $l=|r|$, models are asked to reconstruct the $i$th masked token in $\mathcal{T}(s,o;l)$ with the $i$th relational token $r_{i}$ using the MLM head. (2) For templates where $l\neq|r|$, since there is no relation between $(s,o)$ under $\mathcal{T}(s,o;l)$, models are asked to reconstruct the NA relation.
comments.explanation: The training logic contains a conceptual error. The paper states that for templates where $l\neq|r|$, 'there is no relation between $(s,o)$ under $\mathcal{T}(s,o;l)$' but this is incorrect. The relation $r$ exists in the image regardless of the template length $l$ - only the template's capacity to represent it changes. The correct logic should be: for templates where $l\neq|r|$, the model is asked to reconstruct the NA relation because the template length doesn't match the actual relation length, not because no relation exists.

comments.title: Inference scoring formula error for NA relations
comments.quote: fically, the score of each relation $r\in\mathcal{P}\cup\{\texttt{NA}\}$ is obtained by the aggregated MLM scores of its composing tokens under the corresponding template: $s(r)=\frac{1}{l}\sum_{i=1}^{l}\log P(\texttt{[MASK]}_{i}=r_{i}|\mathcal{T}(s,o;l))$, where $l=|r|$. Intuitively,
comments.explanation: The scoring formula incorrectly states 'where $l=|r|$' for all relations. For NA relations, the paper defines fixed lengths: *irrelevant* (1 token), *no relation* (2 tokens), *no relation with* (3 tokens). The scoring should use the template length $l$ (1, 2, or 3) for NA relations, not $|r|$. The formula should specify that $l$ is the template length, and for NA relations, $r$ consists of the corresponding NA tokens.

comments.title: Uniqueness constraint requires explicit statement
comments.quote: Given an image $I$ and its region proposals $\mathcal{R}=\{v_{1},v_{2},\ldots,v_{n}\}$, visual sub-prompt aims to uniquely mark the image regions with natural visual makers. Interestingly, we note that colored bounding boxes are widely used to uniquely mark objects in images for visualization in the literature. Inspired by this, we bridge the image regions and query text through a set of colors $\mathcal{C}$, where each color $c_{i}=(c_{v}^{i},c_{w}^{i})\in\mathcal{C}$ is defined by its visual appearance $c_{v}^{i}$ (e.g., RGB ($255$, $0$, $0$)) and color text $c_{w}^{i}$ (e.g., red). Then we mark each region proposal $v_{i}$ in the image with a unique color $c_{v}^{i}$ for grounding, resulting in a set of colored image proposals $\Psi(\mathcal{R};\mathcal{C})$, where $\Psi(\cdot)$ denotes visual sub-prompt.
comments.explanation: The uniqueness claim requires that the mapping from regions to colors be injective, which necessitates |𝒞| ≥ |ℛ|. This constraint is not stated in the text. For a counterexample, if an image has 20 region proposals but only 16 distinct colors are available, the pigeonhole principle guarantees at least two regions will share the same color, violating uniqueness. Add the constraint 'where |𝒞| ≥ |ℛ|' after the definition of 𝒞 and specify how cases with n > |𝒞| are handled.

comments.title: Visual sub-prompt function requires formal definition
comments.quote: resulting in a set of colored image proposals $\Psi(\mathcal{R};\mathcal{C})$, where $\Psi(\cdot)$ denotes visual sub-prompt.
comments.explanation: The function Ψ is introduced without formal definition. The notation suggests a mapping Ψ: ℛ × 𝒞 → ℐ' where ℐ' is the space of modified images, but the transformation mechanism is unspecified. The text mentions 'coloring the object with solid blocks and segmentation masks' but doesn't define this mathematically. Provide the formal definition: 'Ψ: ℛ × 𝒞 → ℐ' where Ψ(v_i, c_i) modifies the pixels in region v_i according to color c_v^i, producing image I' ∈ ℐ''.

comments.title: Zero-shot claim needs stronger justification
comments.quote: Equipped with CPT, VL-PTMs can readily perform zero-shot visual grounding without any labeled data, since the cross-modal representations of colors and their composition with other concepts (e.g., objects, attributes and relations) have been well learned by VL-PTMs during pre-training.
comments.explanation: The reasoning chain 'pre-training learns representations → CPT can readily perform zero-shot grounding' lacks formal justification. The fact that VL-PTMs learn cross-modal representations during pre-training does not logically entail that CPT can 'readily perform' zero-shot visual grounding. The authors need to either provide a theoretical analysis showing how the pre-trained representations directly enable zero-shot grounding, or present empirical evidence demonstrating zero-shot capability before any fine-tuning.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated standard deviation reduction claim
comments.quote: Notably, CPT achieves significantly smaller standard deviation than fine-tuning. For example, CPT-Blk achieves  $73.8\%$  relative standard deviation reduction on average with one shot in RefCOCO evaluation.
comments.explanation: The claim of 73.8% relative standard deviation reduction lacks mathematical definition and verification. Add the formula for relative standard deviation reduction: ((SD_finetuning - SD_CPT) / SD_finetuning) × 100% and show the calculation with actual standard deviation values from Table 1 because without the underlying standard deviation numbers and the exact computation method, readers cannot verify this substantial stability claim.

comments.title: Missing evidence for color-based expression interference
comments.quote: We note that CPT-Blk slightly underperforms fine-tuning with 16 shots in RefCOCO+ evaluation. The reason is that RefCOCO+ has more color-based expressions (e.g., the person in red shirt and blue hat), which can disturb our color-based CPT.
comments.explanation: The explanation for CPT-Blk's underperformance on RefCOCO+ attributes it to color-based expressions but provides no quantitative evidence. Add statistical analysis showing the correlation between the frequency of color-based expressions in RefCOCO+ queries and the performance gap, or include ablation experiments demonstrating the interference effect because the current explanation remains speculative without empirical support.

comments.title: Unexplained micro performance drop in predicate classification
comments.quote: We note that while the macro performance of CPT monotonically increases as the shot number grows, the micro performance drops first in 1- and 4-shot settings. This is due to the distribution gap between the balanced training set (i.e., K shot for each relation) and the long-tail test set. Since the relations in the pre-training corpora also follow a long-tail distribution, CPT can achieve a high starting point for micro performance.
comments.explanation: The explanation contains a logical inconsistency. The text attributes the micro performance drop to a distribution gap between balanced training and long-tail test sets, but then immediately states that pre-training corpora also follow a long-tail distribution, which should help CPT achieve a 'high starting point for micro performance.' This creates a contradiction: the same long-tail distribution characteristic is presented as both the cause of performance degradation and the reason for good initial performance. The explanation needs to be reformulated to clarify the mechanism.

comments.title: Mathematical notation inconsistency in probability expression
comments.quote: $P(v=v_{i}|\mathcal{R},q)=P(\texttt{[MASK]}=c_{w}^{i}|\Psi(\mathcal{R};\mathcal{C}),\mathcal{T}_{g}(q))=\frac{\exp(\mathbf{h}_{\texttt{[MASK]}}^{\top}\mathbf{c}_{w}^{i})}{\sum_{e_{j}\in\mathcal{C}}\exp(\mathbf{h}_{\texttt{[MASK]}}^{\top}\mathbf{c}_{w}^{j})},
comments.explanation: The left side $P(v=v_i|\mathcal{R},q)$ suggests predicting region indices, while the right side $P(\texttt{\_\_\_}=c_w^i|\cdot)$ predicts color words. The denominator uses $\sum_{e_j \in \mathcal{C}}$ but sums over $\mathbf{c}_w^j$, mixing notation. Rewrite equation (1) as: $P(v=v_{i}|\mathcal{R},q)=\frac{\exp(\mathbf{h}_{\texttt{\_\_\_}}^{\top}\mathbf{c}_{w}^{i})}{\sum_{j=1}^{|\mathcal{C}|}\exp(\mathbf{h}_{\texttt{\_\_\_}}^{\top}\mathbf{c}_{w}^{j})}$ where $\mathbf{c}_w^i$ is the embedding of the color word associated with region $v_i$, and clarify that $\mathcal{C}$ is the set of color words.

comments.title: Color-region mapping function is undefined
comments.quote: Then we mark each region proposal $v_{i}$ in the image with a unique color $c_{v}^{i}$ for grounding, resulting in a set of colored image proposals $\Psi(\mathcal{R};\mathcal{C})$
comments.explanation: The paper claims to mark each region v_i with a unique color c_v^i, but the mapping function from regions to colors is not defined. Is it a bijection f: ℛ → 𝒞, an injection, or an arbitrary assignment? The uniqueness property depends on this mapping. For example, if f(v_i) = c_{(i mod |𝒞|)}, uniqueness fails when n > |𝒞|. Add 'where f: ℛ → 𝒞 is a bijective mapping' or specify the exact assignment rule used.

comments.title: Inconsistent standard deviation reporting in zero-shot results
comments.explanation: The paper explicitly states: 'For a robust and comprehensive evaluation, we report mean results over 5 random training set splits, as well as the standard deviation.' However, the zero-shot CPT results (CPT-Blk and CPT-Seg) are reported without standard deviations, while the Random baseline includes them. This violates the stated evaluation protocol. Add standard deviation values for all zero-shot CPT results to match the Random baseline and maintain consistency with the evaluation protocol.

comments.title: Table formatting error in CPT-Seg row placement
comments.explanation: The CPT-Seg row for 16-shot setting is misaligned - the 'Shot' column contains 'CPT-Seg' instead of '16', and the 'Model' column is empty. This creates ambiguity about whether these are the 16-shot results. Rewrite the row as '| 16 | CPT-Seg | 45.3 ± 1.8 | 53.3 ± 3.0 | 37.5 ± 1.3 | 44.8 ± 0.9 | 52.5 ± 1.2 | 36.6 ± 1.2 | 51.0 ± 2.6 | 51.4 ± 2.8 |' to properly align with the table structure.

comments.title: Missing standard deviation in CPT-Seg 16-shot results
comments.explanation: The CPT-Seg 16-shot results are missing the standard deviation for the last value (51.4), while all other values in that row include standard deviations. This violates the paper's protocol of reporting both mean and standard deviation. Add the missing standard deviation value as '| 51.4 ± X.X |' where X.X is the appropriate standard deviation value.

comments.title: Template notation inconsistency
comments.quote:
mathcal{T}_r(s,o) = [\mathrm{CLS}]$  The  $s_w$  in  $c_w^i$  color is [MASK] the  $o_w$  in  $c_w^i$  color [SEP]
